keyMyths.title: The Ten Courts of Hell
keyMyths.description: Diyu is structured into ten courts, each ruled by a judge who presides over different aspects of punishment and judgment. Souls are brought before these judges to atone for their sins committed in life, with the severity of punishment determined by their deeds.

keyMyths.title: Reincarnation and the Wheel of Dharma
keyMyths.description: After serving their sentences in Diyu, souls are given the opportunity to reincarnate into one of six realms: gods, demigods, humans, animals, hungry ghosts, and hell beings. The realm they enter depends on their accumulated karma.

keyMyths.title: Ancestor Tablets and Offerings
keyMyths.description: Living descendants maintain a connection with their ancestors through ancestor tablets, which are housed in ancestral halls or on home altars. Regular offerings of food, incense, and paper money are made to honor and appease the spirits of the deceased, ensuring their well-being in the afterlife.

keyMyths.title: The Hungry Ghost Festival (Yulanpen)
keyMyths.description: During the seventh month of the lunar calendar, the gates of Diyu are believed to open, allowing ghosts to roam the earth. The Hungry Ghost Festival is a time for families to provide offerings and perform rituals to appease these wandering spirits and prevent them from causing harm.

keyMyths.title: The Role of Ksitigarbha Bodhisattva
keyMyths.description: Ksitigarbha Bodhisattva is revered as the savior of beings in Diyu, vowing to empty the hells of all suffering souls before achieving enlightenment. He is often depicted descending into Diyu to offer guidance and solace to those undergoing punishment.

sources.name: Stephen F. Teiser, 'The Scripture on the Ten Kings'
sources.description: Scholarly analysis of a key text detailing the bureaucratic structure of Diyu and the judgment of souls.
sources.title: Stephen F. Teiser, 'The Scripture on the Ten Kings'

sources.name: James L. Watson, 'Death Ritual in Late Imperial and Modern China'
sources.description: Examines the evolution and significance of death rituals, including ancestor worship, in Chinese society.
sources.title: James L. Watson, 'Death Ritual in Late Imperial and Modern China'

sources.name: Emily M. Ahern, 'The Cult of the Dead in a Chinese Village'
sources.description: An anthropological study of how ancestor worship functions at the village level, highlighting its social and religious importance.
sources.title: Emily M. Ahern, 'The Cult of the Dead in a Chinese Village'

sources.name: Alan Cole, 'Mothers and Sons in Chinese Buddhism'
sources.description: Explores the role of filial piety and ancestral veneration in Buddhist practice and the cult of Ksitigarbha.
sources.title: Alan Cole, 'Mothers and Sons in Chinese Buddhism'

sources.name: The Book of Rites (Liji)
sources.description: A primary Confucian text outlining proper ritual conduct, including practices related to mourning and ancestor veneration.
sources.title: The Book of Rites (Liji)
